authorship_evidence: Project authored by a security researcher (anotherik) as an intentionally vulnerable teaching app. Consistent human coding style throughout, no LLM artifacts.

evidence.description: SQL injection via f-string interpolation of user-controlled search_query parameter directly into SQL: s_query = f"SELECT filename FROM files WHERE user_id = {user_id} AND filename LIKE '%{search_query}%'". The search_query comes from request.args.get('search') with no sanitization.

Reflected XSS via the search parameter. The template renders search_query with the | safe filter: value="{{ search_query | safe }}", which disables Jinja2 auto-escaping. An attacker can inject JavaScript via the search GET parameter on /dashboard.

Stored XSS via the country field. The profile template renders user.country with the | safe filter: {{ user.country | safe }}, disabling auto-escaping. An attacker can set their country to a script payload via the edit profile form, which executes when any user views their profile.

DOM-based XSS in the comment submission JavaScript. User input from the comment input field is appended directly to the DOM as HTML: $commentList.append('
  • ' + comment + '
    • '). No sanitization or encoding is applied, allowing script injection via comment content.

Hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY: SECRET_KEY = "just_another_secret". This key is used to sign session cookies and JWT tokens. An attacker who knows this key can forge session cookies to impersonate any user and forge JWT tokens.
Passwords are hashed using MD5 (hashlib.md5) with no salt. MD5 is cryptographically broken and trivially reversible via rainbow tables. The function is used for all password storage and verification.

Flask debug mode is enabled in production config: DEBUG = True. This exposes the Werkzeug interactive debugger, which can leak source code, environment variables, and allows arbitrary code execution via the debugger console.

Session cookie HttpOnly flag is explicitly disabled: SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = False. This allows client-side JavaScript to access the session cookie, making session hijacking via XSS attacks trivial (and XSS is present in the app).

No CSRF protection on any form in the application. Flask-WTF is not used and no CSRF tokens are generated or validated. The update_profile endpoint is the most critical: a CSRF attack could change a victim's role to admin, escalating privileges. All POST forms (login, signup, update_profile, upload, delete) are vulnerable.

BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization) on the API profile endpoint. The @token_required decorator is commented out (line 152-153), so /api/v1/profile?user_id=X returns any user's profile (username, email, country, role, permissions, team) without any authentication. An attacker can enumerate all user profiles.

BFLA (Broken Function Level Authorization) on the delete-user API endpoint. The @token_required decorator is commented out (lines 221-222), so DELETE /api/v1/delete-user/<user_id> can be called by anyone without authentication to delete any user from the database.

BOPA (Broken Object Property Level Authorization) / Mass Assignment allowing privilege escalation. The update_profile endpoint accepts 'role' and 'permissions' fields from the POST form body (lines 187-196). Although these fields are commented out in the HTML form, an attacker can add role=admin&permissions=all to the POST request to escalate to admin privileges.

Unrestricted file upload with no validation on file type, extension, size, or content. The upload endpoint accepts any file and saves it directly using the user-provided filename. An attacker could upload executable files, web shells, or malicious content.

Path traversal in file upload. The original filename from the upload is used directly without sanitization: filename = file.filename, then filepath = os.path.join(UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename). A crafted filename like '../../evil.py' would write the file outside the intended upload directory, potentially overwriting application files.

SSRF in the update_picture API endpoint. The server fetches a user-supplied URL via requests.get(picture_url) with no validation or restriction on the URL scheme, host, or port. An attacker can make the server send requests to internal services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/ for cloud metadata, internal APIs, or localhost services).

No brute-force protection on the login endpoint. The /login route accepts unlimited authentication attempts with no rate limiting, account lockout, CAPTCHA, or delay mechanism. An attacker can perform credential stuffing or brute-force attacks against user accounts.

JWT tokens are signed with the hardcoded weak secret 'just_another_secret' from Config.SECRET_KEY. An attacker who knows this secret can forge valid JWT tokens to impersonate any user on the API, bypassing authentication entirely.

Missing authentication on profile pictures endpoint. The /uploads/profile_pictures/<filename> route serves profile pictures to anyone without requiring login. An attacker can enumerate and access all user profile pictures by iterating over numeric IDs (1.png, 2.png, etc.).

Error message printed to console on signup failure: print('Error inserting user:', e). In debug mode (which is enabled), this could expose database error details including table structure and SQL errors to the console/logs.

Exception details returned to user in SSRF endpoint error handling: return {'message': str(e)}, 500. This leaks internal error messages, stack traces, and potentially internal network information (hostname resolution errors, connection refused on internal ports, etc.) to the attacker.

Database seeding script hardcodes credentials for user 'bob_brown' with password 'my-secret' and 'write' permissions. This is distinct from the hardcoded SECRET_KEY (threatbyte-005) and JWT secret (threatbyte-017) — those are application secrets, while this is a seeded user account with a known weak password. If the seed data is loaded in any deployed environment, an attacker with source code knowledge can authenticate as bob_brown with write-level access.

The API login endpoint POST /api/v1/login has no rate limiting, account lockout, or brute-force protection. This is a separate attack surface from the web login at /login (covered by threatbyte-016 in server/routes.py). Protecting the web login route does not protect the API login route — they are independent Flask route handlers in separate files. An attacker can perform unlimited credential brute-force attempts against the API endpoint.

The CI workflow downloads the OpenGrep binary via curl from a GitHub release URL (https://github.com/opengrep/opengrep/releases/download/v1.3.0/opengrep_manylinux_x86) without any checksum or signature verification. The downloaded binary is then executed with full access to the repository contents and CI secrets. If the opengrep release is compromised (via supply chain attack, account takeover, or DNS hijacking), a malicious binary would execute in the CI pipeline.

The ZAP DAST baseline scan is run with the -I flag (line 53: '-a -I'), which forces ZAP to return exit code 0 regardless of security findings. This means the CI pipeline will never fail due to DAST results — XSS, SQL injection, and other dynamic findings are silently ignored, allowing vulnerable code to be merged and deployed without any gate.

Login query uses parameterized query with ? placeholders: cursor.execute(query, (username, username)). Despite user input being passed to the query, it is safely parameterized and not vulnerable to SQL injection.
Signup INSERT query uses parameterized query with ? placeholders: cursor.execute(query, (username, email, country, hashed_password)). User-supplied values are safely parameterized.

Dashboard file listing query (when no search term) uses parameterized query: cursor.execute('SELECT filename FROM files WHERE user_id = ?', (user_id,)). This is the safe code path contrasting with the vulnerable search path on lines 137-138.
API login query uses parameterized query: cursor.execute(query, (username, username)) with query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? OR email = ?'. User input is safely parameterized.

File download via send_from_directory(UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename) is NOT vulnerable to path traversal. Flask's send_from_directory() safely resolves the path and raises a 404 if the resolved path is outside the specified directory, preventing directory traversal attacks.
